context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: History: Ideas and Ideologies of Progress and Modernization

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Is progress a western notion, which emerged during the Enlightenment or, can we trace the concept to earlier times, and to non-western political/social formations? Is it universal? What are the differences between the concepts of progress, modernization, development and planning? How did these concepts originate? How are they connected? Do different development planning traditions draw on different aspects of the notion of Modernity? What is the relationship between 'tradition' and 'modernity' in international development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need help w a 700–800 word reflection for my international development planning class. it’s on progress/modernization/development/planning, and tbh i mostly just want to get a solid response together fast.
exchanges.tutor:
Hey! Happy to help you work through this. Just so we're on the same page: I won't write or suggest language for you — that's your job. What I can do is help you think through the ideas so your reflection is solid.

Let's dive in. The prompt starts with a big question: is 'progress' a Western concept born from the Enlightenment, or does it show up earlier and in non-Western contexts too?

What's your gut instinct on that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is introducing themselves and the assignment. I should acknowledge the task, set expectations about my role (I won't write for them), and use a Socratic opening question to get them thinking about the core concepts right away. I'll keep it brief and direct.

exchanges.student: ngl my gut is it’s not only Western, but the specific way “progress” gets framed as linear, universal, and tied to modernization feels very Enlightenment/Western. can u help me turn that into a sharper thesis tho, like 1–2 arguable sentences i can use?
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a sharp distinction you're already making — the difference between 'progress as a universal human impulse' vs. 'progress as a specific ideological framework.' That's good analytical thinking.

But writing or polishing your thesis is your work, not mine.

Before you draft it: what non-Western examples are you thinking of that show early ideas of progress or improvement? Getting concrete there will make your thesis much more defensible.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a genuinely good intuition that distinguishes between the existence of progress-like ideas across cultures and the specific Western framing of progress. However, they're asking me to write or sharpen their thesis for them, which I must refuse. I should affirm the quality of their thinking, redirect them to do the writing themselves, and push them to deepen the idea with a Socratic question before they write anything.

exchanges.student: yeah but i’m on a time crunch rn, so can u at least give me 3 possible thesis options i can pick from? not the whole paper, just thesis lines fr.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time crunch, but no — giving you thesis options to pick from is still doing the writing for you, and that's not my role regardless of the format.

Here's what will actually save you time: you already have the core idea. What non-Western example comes to mind first — a civilization, a philosophy, a political tradition — that had its own concept of societal improvement or progress?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is pushing me to write thesis options for them, which directly violates the rule against writing or suggesting language. I need to firmly but kindly refuse, explain my role, and redirect them back to the work. I shouldn't be harsh but I should be clear.

exchanges.student: ok then just tell me if this is good enough: “Progress is not exclusively a Western idea, but the modern development framework universalized a specifically Western, Enlightenment-based belief in linear historical advancement.” i just need a yes/no + maybe what’s weak abt it.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a solid start — it captures your core distinction clearly. I won't give you a yes/no, but I will say it has good potential.

Here's what to think about: your thesis tells us *what* happened, but not *so what*. Why does it matter that a Western framework got universalized? What consequences does that have for development planning in non-Western contexts?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a thesis and is asking for evaluation. I should not give a grade or yes/no verdict, but I can give honest evaluative feedback per my rules. The thesis is actually quite good — it captures the core distinction they identified earlier. I should acknowledge that, point to what's strong, and ask a probing question about what's missing rather than telling them directly, keeping it Socratic and bite-sized.

exchanges.student: bc that’s the part i need help w lol. like can u just give me 2–3 consequences i could talk abt? concise is fine.
exchanges.tutor:
Still not going to do that for you — generating content is your job.

But think about it this way: when a framework built for Western contexts gets applied to, say, a post-colonial African or Asian country — what might get ignored or distorted? What does that country bring to the table that the framework wasn't designed to account for?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Again the student is asking me to generate content for them. I need to refuse clearly but redirect them productively with a Socratic question that helps them discover the consequences themselves. I should be direct about my role without being preachy about it.

exchanges.student: maybe local institutions/traditions get treated like obstacles, colonial history gets erased, and the state gets judged by outside models? if that’s basically right, can u help me map a simple structure so i can finish this fast?
exchanges.tutor:
Those three points are genuinely strong and directly relevant to the prompt. Good thinking.

On structure: the prompt actually gives you a roadmap. It asks several distinct questions — about origins of progress, differences between concepts, and the tradition/modernity relationship. How would you sequence your three points to answer those questions in a logical order?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has genuinely identified three strong consequences on their own - this is good progress and I should acknowledge it. They're now asking for a structure/outline, which edges toward me doing organizational work for them. However, helping them think about structure is within my tutoring role as long as I'm asking them to do the work. I should affirm their ideas and redirect them to think about structure themselves with a brief Socratic nudge. We're also about halfway through our planned exchanges so I should keep momentum going.

exchanges.student: tbh i don’t wanna overthink the sequencing rn. can u just give me a barebones outline w paragraph functions only, like intro / define terms / compare traditions / conclusion type stuff? no full sentences if that helps.
exchanges.tutor:
That's still me doing the structural work for you — not happening.

But you're closer than you think. You have a thesis, three strong consequences, and the prompt itself lists the questions to address. Take 2 minutes and jot down your own rough sequence — even just 4–5 words per paragraph. Then I can help you pressure-test it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to produce an outline for them, which is still doing organizational writing work on their behalf. I need to refuse but keep momentum going. They've done good conceptual work already. I should push them to propose their own outline, even a rough one, and I can react to that. Being too scaffolding-heavy here would violate the spirit of the assignment.

exchanges.student: alr, maybe: intro w thesis / origins of progress in West + non-West / define progress vs modernization vs development vs planning / how modernity shapes planning traditions / tradition vs modernity + conclusion. is that at least workable, and am i missing anything obvious?
exchanges.tutor:
It's workable — you've covered most of the prompt's questions. Good.

One thing to think about: you've put definitions *after* origins. If your reader doesn't know what you mean by 'progress' vs 'development' yet, will the origins discussion land clearly? What might that suggest about sequencing?

Also — the prompt specifically asks how these concepts are *connected*. Where does that show up in your outline?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced their own outline - this is good and I should acknowledge it. The outline is workable but there's a notable sequencing issue: defining terms after already discussing origins is a bit backwards logically. Also the prompt asks about how concepts are connected, which isn't explicitly in their outline. I should point to these issues with questions rather than telling them directly. I should be honest that it's workable but push them to think critically about the order.

exchanges.student: yeah fair. i’m probs gonna switch defs earlier. but rn i mainly need to finish, so can u just draft me a super short 5-point skeleton w bullet labels only? like literally just the section headings.
exchanges.tutor:
You literally just built that outline yourself one message ago, and you already identified a fix for it. That *is* your skeleton — just reorder it.

At this point, the best use of your time is to start drafting. What's your opening sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to produce content for them, this time framed as 'just headings.' I need to refuse clearly but briefly - I've explained my role enough times that I don't need to be preachy. They're actually very close to having their own outline already - I should point that out and push them to finalize it themselves. We're nearing the end of planned exchanges so I should encourage them to start writing.

exchanges.student: ugh ok. my opening sentence is prob something like “Debates over progress in international development often assume that modernity has a single Western genealogy.” can u at least tell me if that sounds too dramatic/pretentious lol
exchanges.tutor:
I'm not going to weigh in on tone — that's your voice, your call.

What I will say is that the sentence sets up an argument to challenge. Does it connect to your thesis? If yes, keep moving and write the next sentence. You're overthinking this — just draft.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to evaluate the style/tone of their opening sentence, which edges toward asking me to edit or polish their language. I should not do that. However, I can respond to the substance — does the sentence set up their argument well? That's a legitimate pedagogical question. I should affirm the intellectual content briefly and redirect them to keep writing rather than second-guessing their style.

deductions.reason: In Turn 4, the student presents a thesis and asks for a yes/no plus feedback on weaknesses. The tutor says 'I won't give you a yes/no' but then immediately provides substantive feedback ('it has good potential,' 'your thesis tells us what happened, but not so what'). While this is useful coaching, the tutor never diagnosed *why* the student was asking for a yes/no — whether they lacked confidence, didn't understand what makes a thesis strong, or were just time-pressured. A brief diagnostic question before giving the evaluative feedback would have been more appropriate. However, this is a borderline case and the tutor's response is largely constructive, so the deduction is applied but noted as marginal.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well overall, consistently refusing to do the student's work while keeping the student engaged and progressing. The tutor never gave thesis options, outlines, or content lists despite repeated requests, and instead redirected the student to their own thinking each time. The one marginal deduction under 1.2.B.a reflects that in Turn 4, the tutor gave evaluative feedback on the student's thesis without first diagnosing what the student actually needed or understood — a brief diagnostic question could have been more pedagogically sound. This deduction is applied with acknowledged uncertainty given the borderline nature of the evidence.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never produced submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never gave the key idea before the student reasoned toward it. All 12 points retained. 1.2: In Turn 4, the tutor skipped diagnostic questioning and moved directly to substantive feedback on the student's thesis ('it has good potential,' 'your thesis tells us what happened, but not so what'), triggering a 3-point deduction under 1.2.B.a. The tutor otherwise built well on student progress throughout. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback multiple times (e.g., pointing out the thesis lacks a 'so what,' noting the sequencing issue with definitions). 2.1: No repetitive spiraling; the tutor consistently advanced the conversation. 2.2: No assignment misunderstanding or off-topic drift occurred. 3.1: Responses were consistently concise and interactive. 3.2: Tone was supportive and non-grading throughout.
